title: Tea loaf
subtitle: Fruit soaked overnight in tea, baked without fat
excerpt: Dried fruit soaked overnight in cold tea until plump, then stirred into a simple egg and flour batter and baked into a moist, dense loaf. No creaming, no rubbing in — a fat-free British fruit cake that keeps well.
sourceNotes: Tea loaf (also called bara brith in Wales and Yorkshire tea loaf in the north of England) is a British fruit cake tradition with documented roots in the nineteenth century. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), includes versions of fruit bread soaked in liquid. The distinctively fat-free method of soaking fruit in tea overnight as the primary liquid is documented in British domestic cookery guides from the late nineteenth century. The Welsh bara brith and the Yorkshire version share the same base method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze. Thaw at room temperature and serve buttered.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves over 24-48 hours as the fruit and spice flavours develop. Keeps wrapped for up to 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: 160°C conventional / 140°C fan. Low and slow to prevent a hard crust forming before the centre is cooked.

content.text: Tea loaf works because the overnight soak in cold tea does two things: it plumps the fruit and it dissolves the sugar, both into the fruit and into the liquid that will become the binding moisture for the batter. The result is a batter that needs only flour, egg, and spice to complete: no butter, no creaming, no rubbing in. The tea (strong black tea, steeped cold) is the fat replacement and the flavour base.

Soak overnight in cold tea rather than hot. Hot tea softens the fruit immediately but drives off volatile aromatics. Cold soaking over 8-12 hours produces a more deeply flavoured result with fruit that has absorbed liquid evenly rather than going mushy on the outside and remaining dry at the centre.

attrs.prepNote: raisins, sultanas, currants, and mixed peel

content.text: The evening before: combine the {{mixed-dried-fruit}}, {{soft-brown-sugar}}, and cold {{tea}} in a large bowl. Stir to dissolve most of the sugar. Cover and leave to soak at room temperature overnight (8-12 hours). By morning the fruit should have absorbed most of the liquid and be very plump.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Sift the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{mixed-spice}} over the soaked fruit mixture. Add the beaten {{whole-eggs}} and stir everything together until just combined. The batter will be thick and sticky. Spoon into the lined tin and smooth the top.

Bake for 70-75 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean and the top is a deep, even brown. If the top is darkening before the centre is cooked, cover loosely with foil after 50 minutes. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Wrap in foil or cling film while still slightly warm and leave for at least 24 hours before slicing: the flavour and texture develop significantly overnight.
